content:
# Luke Chapter 19 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 111-113 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_019_page_001.jpg, LUK_019_page_002.jpg, LUK_019_page_003.jpg

---

**1** Jesus entered Jericho and was passing through the city. **2** A man named Zacchaeus was there. He was a chief tax collector and very wealthy. **3** He was trying to see who Jesus was, but he could not see over the crowd because he was short. **4** So he ran ahead and climbed a sycamore-fig tree in order to see Jesus, who was about to pass that way. **5** When Jesus reached the spot, he looked up and said to him, "Zacchaeus, come down immediately. I must stay at your house today." **6** So Zacchaeus hurried down and welcomed him joyfully. **7** When the people saw this, they all began to grumble, saying, "He has gone to be the guest of a sinner."

**8** But Zacchaeus stood up and said to the Lord, "Look, Lord — I am giving half of my possessions to the poor, and if I have cheated anyone out of anything, I am paying back four times as much." **9** Jesus said to him, "Today salvation has come to this household, because this man too is a son of Abraham. **10** For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost."

**11** While the people were listening to this, Jesus went on to tell a parable, because he was near Jerusalem and they thought the kingdom of God was going to appear immediately. **12** He said: "A man of noble birth went to a distant country to be appointed king and then return. **13** So he called ten of his servants and gave them ten minas. 'Put this money to work,' he told them, 'until I come back.'

**14** "But his subjects hated him and sent a delegation after him to say, 'We do not want this man to reign over us.'

**15** "When he returned, having been appointed king, he ordered the servants to whom he had given the money to be called in, so that he could find out what they had earned with it. **16** The first one came and said, 'Sir, your mina has earned ten more minas.' **17** His master replied, 'Well done, good servant! Because you have been trustworthy in a very small matter, you will have authority over ten cities.' **18** The second came and said, 'Sir, your mina has produced five minas.' **19** His master answered, 'You will be put in charge of five cities.'

**20** "Then another servant came and said, 'Sir, here is your mina. I kept it wrapped up in a cloth. **21** I was afraid of you, because you are a harsh man. You withdraw what you did not deposit, and you harvest what you did not plant.' **22** His master replied, 'I will judge you by your own words, you worthless servant! So you knew that I am a harsh man, withdrawing what I did not deposit and harvesting what I did not plant? **23** Then why did you not put my money in the bank, so that when I returned I could have collected it with interest?'

**24** "Then he said to those standing by, 'Take the mina away from him and give it to the one who has ten minas.' **25** They said to him, 'Sir, he already has ten minas!' **26** He replied, 'I tell you, everyone who has will be given more, but the one who has nothing — even what he has will be taken away from him. **27** Now as for those enemies of mine who did not want me to be their king — bring them here and execute them in front of me.'"

**28** After saying this, Jesus went on ahead, going up toward Jerusalem. **29** As he approached Bethphage and Bethany, at the place called the Mount of Olives, he sent two of his disciples ahead, **30** saying, "Go into the village ahead of you. As you enter it, you will find a colt tied there that no one has ever ridden. Untie it and bring it here. **31** If anyone asks you, 'Why are you untying it?' say, 'The Lord needs it.'"

**32** Those who were sent went and found everything just as he had told them. **33** As they were untying the colt, its owners said to them, "Why are you untying the colt?" **34** They replied, "The Lord needs it." **35** They brought it to Jesus, threw their cloaks on the colt, and put Jesus on it. **36** As he rode along, people spread their cloaks on the road.

**37** As he was approaching the place where the road goes down from the Mount of Olives, the whole crowd of disciples began to praise God joyfully and with loud voices for all the miracles they had seen, **38** saying, "Blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!"

**39** Some of the Pharisees in the crowd said to Jesus, "Teacher, rebuke your disciples!" **40** He answered, "I tell you, if they kept silent, the very stones would cry out."

**41** As he drew near and saw the city, he wept over it, **42** saying, "If only you had recognized on this day what would bring you peace — but now it is hidden from your eyes. **43** For the days are coming upon you when your enemies will build a siege wall around you, encircle you, and press in on you from every side. **44** They will level you to the ground — you and the children within your walls. They will not leave one stone standing on another within you, because you did not recognize the time of God's coming to you."

**45** Then he entered the temple and began driving out those who were selling things there, **46** saying to them, "It is written: 'My house will be a house of prayer,' but you have turned it into a den of robbers."

**47** Every day he was teaching in the temple. The chief priests, the scribes, and the leading men of the people were trying to kill him, **48** but they could not find any way to do it, because all the people hung on his every word.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **19:1-4 — Zacchaeus and the sycamore tree**: Zacchaeus is identified as an ARCHITELŌNĒS (architelōnēs, "chief tax collector") — a term found only here in all of Greek literature. This was not merely a man who collected taxes but the head of a regional tax-farming operation. Jericho was a major customs hub (balsam trade, border crossing), so Zacchaeus would have presided over considerable revenue — and considerable public resentment. He was PLOUSIOS (plousios, "wealthy"), which, given the preceding story of the rich ruler, immediately signals a test case: can a wealthy man enter the kingdom? His attempt to see Jesus is described with vivid physical detail: he was MIKROS TĒ HĒLIKIA (mikros tē hēlikia, "small in stature"), so he ran ahead and climbed a SYKOMOREAN (sykomorean, "sycamore-fig tree") — a tree with low-spreading branches, easy to climb but undignified for a man of wealth and position. Everything about Zacchaeus — running, climbing a tree, his short stature — strips him of the social respectability his wealth had purchased.

- **19:5-7 — "I must stay at your house"**: Jesus' words carry divine necessity: SĒMERON GAR EN TŌ OIKŌ SOU DEI ME MEINAI (sēmeron gar en tō oikō sou dei me meinai, "for today I must stay in your house"). The verb DEI ("it is necessary") in Luke's Gospel consistently signals divine purpose (cf. 2:49, 4:43, 9:22, 13:33, 17:25, 24:7, 24:26, 24:44). Jesus does not ask permission — he announces a divine appointment. The crowd's reaction — DIEGOGGYZON (diegoggyzon, "they grumbled" or "they muttered") — uses the same verb applied to Israel's murmuring against God in the wilderness (LXX of Exodus 16:7-8). They complain that Jesus has gone to be the guest of a HAMARTŌLOS ANĒR (hamartōlos anēr, "sinful man"). The irony is thick: the chapter opened with Jesus saying the Son of Man came "to seek and save the lost," and now the crowd objects when he does exactly that.

- **19:8-10 — Zacchaeus's response and salvation**: Zacchaeus's declaration is startling in its generosity: TA HĒMISEIA MOU TŌN HUPARCHONTŌN DIDŌMI TOIS PTŌCHOIS (ta hēmiseia mou tōn huparchontōn didōmi tois ptōchois, "I am giving half of my possessions to the poor"). The present tense DIDŌMI suggests a present resolution, not a future promise. Where the rich ruler was told to sell everything and could not, Zacchaeus voluntarily gives half — plus fourfold restoration (TETRAPLOUN, tetraploun) to anyone he has defrauded, which exceeds the legal requirement (Exodus 22:1 requires fourfold restitution only for stolen oxen; Numbers 5:7 requires the principal plus one-fifth). Jesus responds not with a command but a declaration: SĒMERON SŌTĒRIA TŌ OIKŌ TOUTŌ EGENETO (sēmeron sōtēria tō oikō toutō egeneto, "today salvation has come to this house"). The word SĒMERON ("today") echoes 19:5 — the day of divine appointment is the day of salvation. Verse 10 provides the theological capstone: ĒLTHEN GAR HO HUIOS TOU ANTHRŌPOU ZĒTĒSAI KAI SŌSAI TO APOLŌLOS (ēlthen gar ho huios tou anthrōpou zētēsai kai sōsai to apolōlos, "for the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost"). The verb ZĒTĒSAI ("to seek") implies active pursuit — God does not wait for the lost to find their way home. He climbs after them, as it were, into their sycamore trees. Zacchaeus answers the question posed by the rich ruler episode: a wealthy man can enter the kingdom, but only when his wealth loses its grip on his heart.

- **19:11-13 — The parable of the minas**: Luke provides an explicit reason for this parable: DIA TO ENGYS EINAI IEROUSALĒM AUTON KAI DOKEIN AUTOUS HOTI PARACHRĒMA MELLEI HĒ BASILEIA TOU THEOU ANAPHAINESTHAI (dia to engys einai Ierousalēm auton kai dokein autous hoti parachrēma mellei hē basileia tou theou anaphainesthai, "because he was near Jerusalem and they thought the kingdom of God was about to appear immediately"). The parable corrects the expectation of an immediate visible kingdom. A MNA (mna, "mina") was worth about 100 drachmas — roughly three months' wages for a laborer. Unlike Matthew's parable of the talents, where different amounts are given according to ability, here each servant receives the same amount. The test is what they do with equal opportunity, not unequal endowment. The instruction PRAGMATEUSASTHE (pragmateusasthe, "do business" or "trade") is a command to productive engagement, not passive preservation.

- **19:14-15 — The rejected king**: The subplot of citizens who send a delegation (PRESBEIAN, presbeian) saying OU THELOMEN TOUTON BASILEUSAI EPH' HĒMAS (ou thelomen touton basileusai eph' hēmas, "we do not want this man to reign over us") has a historical parallel: when Archelaus, son of Herod the Great, went to Rome in 4 BC to receive his kingdom, a Jewish delegation of fifty followed to protest his appointment. Jesus' audience would have recognized the allusion. But the parable transcends the historical reference: the rejected nobleman is Christ himself, whose kingship is refused by his own people.

- **19:16-19 — Faithful servants rewarded**: The first servant's mina has earned DEKA MNAS (deka mnas, "ten minas") — a tenfold return. The master's response — EUGE AGATHE DOULE (euge agathe doule, "well done, good servant") — praises character (AGATHE, "good") before praising performance. The reward is authority: EXOUSIAN ECHŌN EPANŌ DEKA POLEŌN (exousian echōn epanō deka poleōn, "having authority over ten cities"). Faithfulness with small things leads to stewardship of great ones. The principle scales proportionally: five minas earn authority over five cities. The rewards are not retirement but greater responsibility — the kingdom is not a place of leisure but of active governance.

- **19:20-23 — The worthless servant**: The third servant returns the mina APOKEIMEĒN EN SOUDARIŌ (apokeimenēn en soudariō, "stored away in a cloth"). A SOUDARION was a facecloth or sweat-cloth — the most minimal form of safekeeping, without even the security of burial. His excuse reveals his theology: EPHOBOUMIN SE HOTI ANTHRŌPOS AUSTĒROS EI (ephoboumēn se hoti anthrōpos austēros ei, "I was afraid of you because you are a harsh man"). The word AUSTĒROS means "strict," "exacting," "severe." The master does not deny the characterization but turns it against the servant: EK TOU STOMATOS SOU KRINŌ SE PONĒRE DOULE (ek tou stomatos sou krinō se ponēre doule, "I judge you out of your own mouth, wicked servant"). If the master is truly as demanding as the servant claims, then the servant's inaction is not caution but negligence. Even the minimum response — depositing the money EPI TRAPEZAN (epi trapezan, "on a table," i.e., with a money-changer or bank) — would have produced SUN TOKŌ (sun tokō, "with interest"). Fear that paralyzes rather than motivates is not reverence but disobedience in disguise.

- **19:24-27 — The principle of accumulation and the fate of enemies**: The bystanders' protest — KYRIE ECHEI DEKA MNAS (kyrie echei deka mnas, "Lord, he already has ten minas!") — expresses the human sense of fairness that the kingdom consistently overturns. The master's principle is stark: TŌ ECHONTI DOTHĒSETAI, APO DE TOU MĒ ECHONTOS KAI HO ECHEI ARTHĒSETAI (tō echonti dothēsetai, apo de tou mē echontos kai ho echei arthēsetai, "to the one who has, more will be given, but from the one who has nothing, even what he has will be taken away"). This is not economic policy but spiritual reality: faithfulness generates capacity for more faithfulness, while unfaithfulness atrophies even existing ability. The parable's final verse is the most severe in Luke's Gospel: TOUS ECHTHROUS MOU TOUTOUS... KATASPHAXATE AUTOUS EMPROSTHEN MOU (tous echthrous mou toutous... katasphaxate autous emprosthen mou, "those enemies of mine... slaughter them before me"). The verb KATASPHAZŌ means "to slaughter" or "to cut down" — language of wholesale execution. Within the parable, it mirrors what Archelaus did upon his return. In Jesus' application, it points to the coming destruction of Jerusalem (foreshadowed in 19:41-44) and, beyond that, to the final judgment.

- **19:28-36 — The triumphal entry preparations**: Jesus sends two disciples with supernaturally precise instructions: they will find a PŌLON (pōlon, "colt") — a young donkey — on which OUDEIS PŌPOTE ANTHRŌPŌN EKATHISEN (oudeis pōpote anthrōpōn ekathisen, "no one has ever sat"). An unridden animal was considered appropriate for sacred use (cf. Numbers 19:2, 1 Samuel 6:7). The password-like exchange — "Why are you untying it?" / "The Lord needs it" — suggests prior arrangement, though Luke presents it as prophetic foreknowledge. The detail that everything was found KATHŌS EIPEN AUTOIS (kathōs eipen autois, "just as he told them") emphasizes Jesus' sovereign control of events as he approaches his death. The spreading of HIMATIA (himatia, "cloaks") on the colt and on the road echoes the coronation of Jehu in 2 Kings 9:13 — a gesture of royal homage.

- **19:37-38 — The disciples' praise**: The praise erupts at a specific location: PROS TĒ KATABASEI TOU OROUS TŌN ELAIŌN (pros tē katabasei tou orous tōn elaiōn, "near the descent of the Mount of Olives") — the point where Jerusalem suddenly becomes visible. The crowd praises God PERI PASŌN HŌN EIDON DYNAMEŌN (peri pasōn hōn eidon dynameōn, "for all the miracles they had seen"). Their acclamation — EULOGĒMENOS HO ERCHOMENOS HO BASILEUS EN ONOMATI KYRIOU (eulogēmenos ho erchomenos ho basileus en onomati kyriou, "blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord") — quotes Psalm 118:26 but adds the word HO BASILEUS ("the king"), making the messianic claim explicit. The cry EIRĒNĒ EN OURANŌ KAI DOXA EN HYPSISTOIS (eirēnē en ouranō kai doxa en hypsistois, "peace in heaven and glory in the highest") echoes the angels' song at Jesus' birth (2:14), but with a crucial change: the angels sang "peace on earth," while the disciples sing "peace in heaven." The peace that was offered to earth has been rejected; it now resides in heaven alone. This subtle shift anticipates the weeping of verses 41-44.

- **19:39-40 — "The stones would cry out"**: The Pharisees demand that Jesus EPITIMĒSON (epitimēson, "rebuke") his disciples — the same verb used when the crowd tried to silence the blind beggar in 18:39. Jesus' response — EAN HOUTOI SIGĒSOUSIN HOI LITHOI KRAXOUSIN (ean houtoi sigēsousin hoi lithoi kraxousin, "if these are silent, the stones will cry out") — may allude to Habakkuk 2:11, where the stones of the wall cry out against injustice. But in this context, with the destruction of Jerusalem looming, the "stones" take on a grimmer resonance: the very stones of the temple and city that will soon be thrown down (19:44) bear witness to the king the city is about to reject.

- **19:41-44 — Jesus weeps over Jerusalem**: The verb EKLAUSEN (eklausen, "he wept") denotes audible weeping, not merely tears. This is one of only two occasions in the Gospels where Jesus weeps (cf. John 11:35). His lament — EI EGNŌS EN TĒ HĒMERA TAUTĒ KAI SU TA PROS EIRĒNĒN (ei egnōs en tē hēmera tautē kai su ta pros eirēnēn, "if you had known on this day — even you — the things that make for peace") — breaks off mid-sentence. The APOSIOPESIS (incomplete sentence) is a mark of overwhelming emotion: Jesus cannot finish the thought because what follows is too terrible. What follows is a detailed prophecy of siege warfare: PERIBALOUSIN... ECHTHHROI SOU CHARAKA SOI (peribalousin... echthroi sou charaka soi, "your enemies will throw up a palisade against you"), PERIKYKLŌSOUSIN SE (perikyklōsousin se, "they will encircle you"), SYNEXOUSIN SE PANTOTHEN (synexousin se pantothen, "they will hem you in on every side"). The verb EDAPHIOUSIN SE (edaphiousin se, "they will dash you to the ground") refers to leveling a city — razing it to its foundations. The phrase OUK APHĒSOUSIN LITHON EPI LITHON EN SOI (ouk aphēsousin lithon epi lithon en soi, "they will not leave stone upon stone in you") was fulfilled literally in 70 AD when Titus's legions destroyed Jerusalem and dismantled the temple. The reason given is devastating in its simplicity: ANTH' HŌN OUK EGNŌS TON KAIRON TĒS EPISKOPĒS SOU (anth' hōn ouk egnōs ton kairon tēs episkopēs sou, "because you did not recognize the time of your visitation"). The word EPISKOPĒ means "visitation" or "inspection" — God coming to his people in person, in the flesh, riding on a donkey, weeping. Jerusalem's destruction was not arbitrary divine punishment but the consequence of failing to recognize God when he stood in their streets.

- **19:45-46 — The temple cleansing**: Luke's account is the most compressed of the Synoptic versions. Jesus drives out TOUS PŌLOUNTAS (tous pōlountas, "those who were selling") — the Sinaiticus text omits "and buying" found in some manuscripts. His scriptural citation weaves together Isaiah 56:7 (HO OIKOS MOU OIKOS PROSEUCHĒS, ho oikos mou oikos proseuchēs, "my house shall be a house of prayer") and Jeremiah 7:11 (SPĒLAION LĒSTŌN, spēlaion lēstōn, "den of robbers"). The word LĒSTĒS does not mean "thief" (KLEPTĒS) but "robber" or "bandit" — someone who uses violence for gain. In Jeremiah's original context, the temple had become a refuge where people retreated after committing injustice, treating it as a safe house rather than a place of genuine worship. Jesus' accusation is not primarily about commercial dishonesty in the temple courts but about the deeper corruption: using religion as a cover for lives that contradict it.

- **19:47-48 — Daily teaching under threat**: Luke establishes the pattern that will dominate the next two chapters: Jesus teaches daily in the temple (ĒN DIDASKŌN TO KATH' HĒMERAN EN TŌ HIERŌ, ēn didaskōn to kath' hēmeran en tō hierō), while the religious leadership seeks to destroy him. The coalition against him includes HOI ARCHIEREIS KAI HOI GRAMMATEIS KAI HOI PRŌTOI TOU LAOU (hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis kai hoi prōtoi tou laou, "the chief priests and the scribes and the leading men of the people") — the full spectrum of institutional power. Yet they cannot act because HO LAOS GAR HAPAS EXEKREMATO AUTOU AKOUŌN (ho laos gar hapas exekremato autou akouōn, "all the people hung on his words listening"). The verb EXEKREMATO, from EKKREMANNYMI, literally means "to hang from" or "to hang upon" — the crowd was suspended, as it were, from his words, captivated and unwilling to let go. The chapter thus ends with an exquisite tension: the city that does not recognize the time of its visitation nevertheless cannot stop listening to the visitor.

- **19:1-48 — The chapter as a whole**: Luke 19 is structured as a journey from the outskirts to the center — from Jericho to the temple — and with each stage, the stakes escalate. In Jericho, Jesus seeks the lost and finds Zacchaeus; on the road, he tells a parable about a rejected king who returns to judge; on the Mount of Olives, he is acclaimed as king but weeps because the acclamation will be short-lived; in the temple, he acts with royal authority, cleansing his Father's house. The chapter answers the parable of the minas with living examples: Zacchaeus is the faithful servant who puts what he has been given to productive use; the religious establishment is the worthless servant who buries its stewardship in ritual while resisting the master's return; and Jerusalem itself is the rebellious citizenry that sent a delegation saying, "We do not want this man to reign over us." The most haunting theme is recognition: Zacchaeus, the despised tax collector, recognizes Jesus and is saved; Jerusalem, the holy city, fails to recognize him and is destroyed. The chapter pivots on the word SĒMERON ("today") — today salvation comes to Zacchaeus's house (19:9), but today Jerusalem does not know the things that belong to its peace (19:42). The same day offers both salvation and judgment, depending entirely on whether the visitation is recognized for what it is.
